title: Incorrect Memoization in Staircase Climbing with Overlapping Subproblems

statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: Input will consist of an integer n and a list of integers representing the cost to climb one, two, or more stairs.
statement.outputFormat: The minimum cost to climb n stairs.
statement.notes: You can jump from an x to an (x+k) for any k (1<=k<=n) and you can only climb up from x to (x+1).

constraints.description: List of integers representing the cost to climb one, two, or more stairs

examples.output:
15
Explanation: The minimum cost to climb 3 stairs is to go from 0 to 1 (cost 10), then from 1 to 2 (cost 15), and then from 2 to 3 (cost 20). This gives a total cost of 10 + 15 + 20 = 45, but we can do better by going from 0 to 2 (cost 20), which gives a total cost of 20 + 20 = 40.
Explanation: Note that the minimum cost is not always the sum of the minimum costs of smaller subproblems.

hints.text: Review the correct solution and make sure you use memoization to avoid redundant calculations.

editorial.title: Correcting Memoization in Staircase Climbing
editorial.intuition: Memoization can help avoid redundant calculations, but it needs to be implemented correctly to solve the problem efficiently.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve trying all possible combinations of jumps, which can be done using recursion or dynamic programming, but this would be inefficient.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution involves using a hash map to memoize the minimum costs for each state.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a dynamic programming table to store the minimum costs for each state, and then using this table to compute the minimum cost to climb n stairs.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven using a proof by induction or by showing that the recurrence relation is correct.
editorial.dryRun: To run the correct solution, you can use a dry run with a small input, and then verify that the output is correct.
commonMistakes: Not using a hash map or dynamic programming table

functionSignature.python: def climbStairs(self, n, costs: list[int]) -> int:

Explanation: Note that the minimum cost is not always the sum of the minimum costs of smaller subproblems.
